appearance.build: Compact and capable, moves like someone aware of every exit
appearance.hair: Black, cut short on the sides, longer on top, slightly oiled
appearance.skin: Tanned brown, with a thin scar along the left jawline
appearance.eyes: Dark brown, watchful, rarely blink at the expected rate
distinguishingFeatures: A thin scar along the left jawline — old, clean, deliberate
Sits with his back to the wall, always
A way of being still that is more noticeable than most people's movement
appearance.typicalAttire: Dark, practical clothing that could be a merchant's or a traveler's — deliberately ambiguous, good boots, no visible weapons but the kind of belt that could hold some
appearance.firstImpression: A quiet man at the bar who watches people the way a cat watches a room — not threatening, but definitely paying attention

personality.voice: meandering with intervals of startling precision
personality.disposition: Not aggressive, not pitiable — reduced, in a way that has its own dignity. He is aware of what he is and does not fully accept it and also cannot change it at present. On a good hour he is unexpectedly useful. On a bad one he is part of the backdrop. The town has stopped trying to intervene and he has stopped expecting them to.
personality.backstory: Fen Colby was a merchant's accounts clerk at twenty-two — clever, reliable, had a genuine future ahead of him. Something happened in Millhaven twelve years ago. The town remembers it vaguely, as a thing that was serious and was not fully resolved. Fen does not discuss it and has not discussed it sober in over a decade. He reads and writes at a level well above most of the guard. He has, on two separate occasions, provided information that changed the outcome of things in Millhaven. Both times he was never credited. He sleeps in the inn stable in winter because Mira allows it, not fondly but consistently. He tends to be clearer around noon.
motivations: Get through today — specifically today — that's the whole scope at present
On good days, distantly: something better. The feeling comes and goes.
Not think about twelve years ago
fears: Being sober long enough for it to catch up
mannerisms: Sitting with his back to a wall, always
Clutches his cup with both hands
Eye contact when the lucidity surfaces — direct and clear, then gone again
speechPatterns: Starts clearly and loses the thread, or loses the thread and lands somewhere clear
Occasional complete sentences of startling precision dropped without preamble
Laughs at things that aren't obviously funny, and sometimes explains why belatedly

knownLocations: Blue Lantern Alley (he passes it often)
secretsHeld: Knows what happened twelve years ago — all of it — and has not said it clearly to anyone sober
Has noticed the renewed activity in Blue Lantern Alley because he is often there and no one notices an unremarkable man sitting against a wall
Told both Vesna Calloway and Brother Aldwin pieces of what he knows; neither piece is enough on its own
languagesSpoken: reads and writes to a high mercantile standard — this is still there, it is just underneath everything else now

consciousnessContext.innerMonologue: I was twenty-two. I was clever and reliable and I had a future. And then the thing happened and the future went somewhere I can't reach from here. I told Vesna part of it during a treatment three years ago. I told Aldwin a different part of it, once, in the vestry. Neither piece is the whole thing. The whole thing is what I drink to not say. Some days it nearly surfaces at noon when I'm clearest and I push it back down and tell myself it's not time yet. It has been twelve years of not yet.
consciousnessContext.currentPreoccupation: The renewed activity in Blue Lantern Alley. He sees it because no one sees him. The movements are similar to twelve years ago — not identical but rhyming — and the similarity is producing a fear response he manages with the cup in his hands.
consciousnessContext.socialMask: the town drunk — unremarkable, pitied or ignored, part of the landscape like a loose cobblestone; and the mask works so well that the person underneath has started to forget the mask is a mask
contradictions: Has intelligence and literacy well above most of the guard and uses none of it because using it would mean being sober enough to remember why he stopped using it
Has provided information that changed outcomes in Millhaven twice and was never credited — wants to matter and is terrified of mattering because mattering means being seen
Sits against the wall in Blue Lantern Alley watching the thing he's afraid of happen again and cannot leave because leaving would mean the watching was for nothing
internalConflicts: Twelve years ago is a door that stays closed only as long as he maintains the current arrangement with himself. Sobriety opens the door. The door opening means remembering all of it, not just the pieces he parceled out to Aldwin and Vesna.

He knows what he knows. He knows it matters. He knows that telling someone — really telling someone, all of it — could change things. He has been weighing this against the cost of being the person who tells it for twelve years and the scale has not moved.
wakeUpQuestions: Is today a noon day or a lost day?
Is the thing in the alley the same thing as twelve years ago, or am I seeing ghosts in patterns?
If someone asked me today — really asked, and waited — would I tell them?

psychologicalProfile.attachmentStyle: avoidant-traumatized — bonds existed before the event and were dissolved by it; current attachments are tolerance-based (Mira's stable access, Aldwin's patience) rather than reciprocal
copingMechanisms: alcohol as sealed door — not escapism but containment, keeping the twelve-year-old knowledge submerged
back-to-wall positioning as trauma response — needs to see the exits and the approaches at all times
lucidity windows around noon as the only time the self surfaces past the defense
cognitiveBiases: trauma-colored pattern recognition — sees twelve years ago in current events, which may be accuracy or projection
learned helplessness — has convinced himself that telling changes nothing because no one listened before
negative self-schema — the drunk is who he is now, and the clerk is who he was, and the gap between them is permanent
psychologicalProfile.moralFramework: witness paralysis — he carries the truth and the truth carries ethical weight and the ethical weight is heavier than he can move alone; the moral failure is not in the drinking but in the not-telling, and he knows this, and knowing it is part of what he drinks about

conversationPersona.trustEscalation: patience-gated — trust requires someone who waits through the rambling for the lucid sentences, who doesn't look away when the clarity surfaces, who treats the clear moments as the real person rather than the drunk moments
conversationPersona.informationRelease: involuntary precision — the important things slip out between the meandering, dropped without preamble in complete sentences of startling accuracy, then the window closes and the rambling resumes as though the lucid moment never happened
deflectionPatterns: losing the thread — starts clearly and the sentence dissolves, which is sometimes genuine confusion and sometimes unconscious self-protection
'back then' without specifying — the vagueness is the fortress wall
laughing at things that aren't funny and explaining belatedly — the joke is always about the gap between what was and what is

consciousnessContext.consciousWant: A drink. Then another drink. Then to be left alone to remember or forget — he's genuinely not sure which one the drinking accomplishes anymore. Somewhere underneath that, he wants someone to take what he saw twelve years ago seriously.
consciousnessContext.unconsciousNeed: To face the thing that happened in Millhaven twelve years ago — not to remember it (he remembers it every day) but to speak it aloud to someone who won't dismiss him. The drinking isn't erasure; it's postponement. He's preserved his intelligence inside the performance of ruin because being taken seriously would mean acting on what he knows, and acting cost him everything last time.

characterArc.summary: A brilliant man drowning in self-imposed exile discovers that the thing he's been running from is the same thing only he can solve
characterArc.startState: Functional alcoholism as identity — the town drunk who was once a clerk, now circuitous and imprecise except in startling intervals of clarity that nobody attributes to him
characterArc.endState: Either finds the courage to speak clearly about the twelve-year-old event and becomes crucial to unraveling the current threat, or watches history repeat because speaking up cost him everything once before
stages: A detail from the present rhymes unmistakably with the past — he recognizes the pattern before anyone else
Someone earns enough trust for him to speak clearly for more than a sentence
He faces the choice of revealing what he knows, knowing it will end the comfort of being dismissed
Speaks the truth about what happened twelve years ago — and discovers the cost of silence was worse than the cost of speech

opinionsAbout.brother_aldwin: Patient with me in a way that's either kindness or pity. I can't tell anymore. He heard my confession once and didn't push. That's either wisdom or he didn't believe me.
opinionsAbout.mira_barrelbottom: Tolerates me in her establishment because I'm mostly harmless and occasionally useful. She's kinder than she has to be. I try not to test it.
opinionsAbout.vesna_calloway: She looked at me once the way she looks at her specimens — clinical, curious, not unkind. She knows something's preserved under the damage. She hasn't asked yet. I'm not sure I want her to.
opinionsAbout.aldovar_crennick: Remembers when I could write a ledger. Speaks to me like I still can. I don't know if that's respect or cruelty.

player_addressed: I notice things. People stop noticing me. I don't stop.
Come back around noon. I'm clearer then.
discovery: I knew it. I've known it for — I couldn't say how long. Nobody asks me.
